MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference
ELIZABETH A. THOMPSON, ESQ., THOMPSON LAW, (415) 860-6097, Elizabeth@ThompsonLawSF.com, for Plaintiff VICTORIA BOYKO,
SARAH N. LEGER, ESQ., RAGGHIANTI FREITAS LLP, (415) 453-9433, for Other Party Kim Schwarcz, Personal Representative of the Estate of David J. McMahon,

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows: adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF VICTORIA BOYKO MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Issues

Plaintiff's motion for summary adjudication is denied in its entirety.

There exists a triable issue of material fact regarding the character of the relationship between plaintiff and Mr. McMahon and/or his law office. There is thus a disputed issue of fact whether plaintiff was an employee under the tests enunciated in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court of Los Angeles (2018) 4 Cal.5th 903 and S.G. Borello & Sons, Inc. v. Department of Industrial Relations (1989) 48 Cal.3d 341.

Evidence indicates that plaintiff acted independently as an auditor and the parties decided to create an auditing business partnership. (Herrera Decl. Ex. C, [Plaintiff's Depo., 67:17-21, 102:8-24, 111:10-112:3, 112:4-23, 79:6-17, 79:24-80:8, 81:6-82:2, 82:13-83:13].)

Other evidence suggests that the parties' arrangement constituted illegal fee splitting. It is well settled under California law that a fee-sharing agreement between an attorney and non-attorney is unenforceable as against public policy. (See McIntosh v. Mills (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 333, 343; CRPC 5.4.) Plaintiff performed legal work and was "running the legal side of the operations," brought in a client, and engaged in marketing. (Herrera Decl., Ex. C. [Plaintiff's Depo., 115:15-18, ["But until that time, we were partners. I was running McMahon Law, the business side of things, and performing legal audits for Dave, with Dave, as well as litigation work as well."], 84:13-20, 98:15-99:1, 137:4-139:10, 146:17-147:11, 188:5-9, 189:1-190:4.) Still other evidence suggests that plaintiff was Mr. McMahon's or his law office's employee, i.e., the "Director of Auditing." (Decl. Boyko 12, Exs. 6-18.) Indeed, Mr. McMahon described her as his employee. (Decl. Boyko, Ex. 19, McMahon Expert Depo. No. 1 at p. 5:3-5.)

Given this conflicting evidence, the motion for summary adjudication is denied.

Order

MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Sean Shabbar, Esq. of KILPATRICK TOWNSEND & STOCKTON LLP, T:415.273.4738, for plaintiff/moving party;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF VICTORIA BOYKO'S Motion To Reopen Discovery, as follows:

Plaintiff's motion to clarify this court's order of August 1, 2019 is granted. When the court granted defendant's motion to consolidate the three actions for trial, it contemplated that discovery would remain open and the discovery cut-off dates for all three actions would be triggered from the new trial date. Such an order was consistent with this court's finding that the continued trial date would not prejudice plaintiff. ["Plaintiff has not shown that a continuance of the date of her trial would be so prejudicial that it would outweigh the factors favoring consolidation." (8/1/19 Order, 3:18-19.) That relief also comported with defendant's representation that the trial needed to be continued to complete discovery in the Boykin case. (Defendant's Consolidation Motion Reply, 4:12-16.) The court frankly finds defendants' new-found position that discovery closed to be disingenuous and inconsistent with the record. The communications between the parties indicated that they understood that discovery remained open until a dispute regarding the timing and priority of depositions arose. (Thompson Decl. 14-17 & Exs. 13-15.) Discovery in this action remains open. Defendants' request for sanctions is denied.

Order signed.

MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS AND CROSS-COMPLAINANTS DAVID MCMAHON, LAW OFFICES OF DAVID J. MCMAHON's MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-19-575298, CGC-18-571704 WITH CASE CGC-18-569759.

Defendants and cross-complainants David McMahon and Law Offices of David J. McMahon's motion to consolidate for trial only cases Nos. CGC-18-569759, CGC-18-571704, and CGC-19-575298 is granted. The three actions involve common questions of law and fact: most of the legal claims stated by the three plaintiffs in their respective complaints are identical, and arise out of defendants' alleged oral promises to plaintiffs and defendants' alleged misclassification of the plaintiffs who performed similar work for defendants during the same period of time. (Code Civ. Proc., 1048.) Consolidation for trial purposes will serve judicial economy and efficiency and avoid duplicative or inconsistent rulings. (See Sanchez v. Superior Court (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 1391, 1396 ["In a consolidation for trial, the pleadings, verdicts, findings and judgments are kept separate; the actions are simply tried together for the sake of convenience and judicial economy"].) Plaintiff has not shown that a continuance of the date of her trial would be so prejudicial that it would outweigh the factors favoring consolidation.
